title: Schema: E-commerce orders
prompt: Design the tables for an online store: customers place orders, each order contains many products with a quantity, and you must record the price at the time of purchase.

sources.label: GeeksforGeeks â€” How to design a relational database for an e-commerce website
Hello Interview â€” Data modeling for system design interviews

keyIdea: Ordersâ†”products is many-to-many, so resolve it with an order_items junction table that carries quantity and the captured unit_price. Store money as DECIMAL/cents.

notes.junction-table: Optimal. order_items resolves the many-to-many between orders and products.
notes.one-to-many-fk: Optimal. customerâ†’orders and orderâ†’order_items are one-to-many via FKs.
notes.foreign-key: Optimal. FKs from ordersâ†’customers and order_itemsâ†’orders/products keep the data valid.
notes.money-decimal: Optimal. Prices and totals must be DECIMAL or integer cents â€” never float.
notes.index-fk: Optimal. Index order_items.order_id and product_id so order/product lookups are fast.
notes.junction-extra-cols: Acceptable/expected. quantity and unit_price live on order_items, not on products.
notes.composite-key: Acceptable. (order_id, product_id) is a fine PK for order_items (or a surrogate id + UNIQUE on the pair).
notes.audit-columns: Acceptable. created_at on orders is standard and useful.
notes.denormalize: Trap. Capturing unit_price on the order is correct (a snapshot), but adding a cached order_total before you need it is premature denormalization.
notes.polymorphic-assoc: Poor. There's nothing polymorphic here; it would only throw away foreign-key integrity.
notes.self-referencing-fk: Poor. No hierarchy in this core model (categories would be a separate concern).

solution.notes: order_items is the junction table. unit_price is copied onto the line so historical orders don't change when product prices do. Index both FKs on order_items.

title: Schema: Social follow graph
prompt: Design tables so users can follow other users (directional), prevent duplicate follows, and let you query a user's followers and who they follow.

sources.label: DataCamp â€” Many-to-many relationships: a guide to database design
Red-Gate â€” Top database schema design interview questions

keyIdea: A follow is a self-referencing many-to-many: one follows table with follower_id and followee_id, a composite PK to block duplicates, and an index on each direction.

notes.junction-table: Optimal. A follows table links users to users.
notes.composite-key: Optimal. PK (follower_id, followee_id) makes a duplicate follow impossible.
notes.foreign-key: Optimal. Both columns are FKs to users.id.
notes.index-fk: Optimal. You query both 'who follows me' and 'who I follow' â€” index both columns.
notes.unique-constraint: Acceptable. Equivalent to the composite PK if you used a surrogate id instead.
notes.composite-index: Acceptable. (follower_id, followee_id) and the reverse serve the two query directions.
notes.self-referencing-fk: Acceptable framing â€” the junction references the same users table on both sides.
notes.money-decimal: Poor. No money in this model.
notes.polymorphic-assoc: Poor. Both sides are users; a plain FK pair is correct.

solution.notes: Composite PK prevents duplicate follows; the CHECK stops self-follows. Index followee_id separately to list a user's followers efficiently.

title: Schema: Hotel room bookings
prompt: Design tables for booking hotel rooms: guests book a room for a date range. Prevent invalid ranges and avoid double-booking the same room for overlapping dates.

sources.label: InterviewQuery â€” Database design interview questions
Red-Gate â€” Top database schema design interview questions

keyIdea: Model rooms, guests, and bookings with FKs; use a CHECK so check_out > check_in; prevent overlaps with a uniqueness/exclusion rule and index the room+date range for lookups.

notes.foreign-key: Optimal. bookings reference both room and guest.
notes.check-constraint: Optimal. CHECK (check_out > check_in) blocks invalid ranges at the schema level.
notes.one-to-many-fk: Optimal. A room has many bookings; a guest has many bookings.
notes.index-fk: Optimal. Index room_id (and the date columns) to find a room's bookings fast.
notes.composite-index: Acceptable. (room_id, check_in, check_out) supports overlap checks.
notes.status-history: Acceptable. Booking status changes (heldâ†’confirmedâ†’cancelled) can be tracked over time.
notes.not-null: Acceptable. Dates, room_id, guest_id are all mandatory.
notes.denormalize: Trap. Don't precompute availability tables before you need them â€” derive from bookings first.
notes.money-decimal: Acceptable only if you add pricing; not the core of overlap prevention.

solution.notes: Prevent overlapping bookings with a DB exclusion constraint (Postgres: EXCLUDE USING gist on room_id + daterange) or enforce in a transaction with a (room_id, check_in, check_out) index. A simple UNIQUE can't express overlap.

title: Schema: Multi-tenant SaaS
prompt: Design a shared-schema multi-tenant app where each customer organization's data must be isolated. Tenants have users and projects; queries must never leak across tenants.

sources.label: Hello Interview â€” Data modeling for system design interviews
InterviewQuery â€” Database design interview questions

keyIdea: Put tenant_id on every tenant-owned table, include it in composite indexes and ideally keys, and scope every query by it. The tenant_id discipline is the whole game.

notes.multi-tenant-key: Optimal. tenant_id on every owned table is the core isolation mechanism.
notes.foreign-key: Optimal. FKs to tenants and within a tenant's data.
notes.composite-index: Optimal. (tenant_id, â€¦) indexes keep per-tenant queries fast and scoped.
notes.denormalize: Poor (premature). Don't duplicate tenant data for speed before measuring.
notes.polymorphic-assoc: Poor. Adds risk and breaks FK integrity â€” dangerous in a multi-tenant isolation context.
notes.self-referencing-fk: Poor. No hierarchy required by the prompt.

solution.notes: tenant_id on every table; UNIQUE(tenant_id, email) so email is unique per tenant, not globally. Lead composite indexes with tenant_id. Belt-and-suspenders: row-level security to prevent cross-tenant leaks.

title: Schema: Blog posts and tags
prompt: Design tables for a blog: authors write posts, and each post can have many tags while each tag applies to many posts.

sources.label: DataCamp â€” Many-to-many relationships: a guide to database design
GeeksforGeeks â€” Database design & modeling interview questions

keyIdea: Postsâ†”tags is many-to-many â†’ a post_tags junction. Authorsâ†’posts is one-to-many. Tags is essentially a lookup table referenced by the junction.

notes.junction-table: Optimal. post_tags links posts and tags.
notes.foreign-key: Optimal. post_tags FKs to posts and tags; posts FK to authors.
notes.index-fk: Optimal. Index both junction FKs to query posts-by-tag and tags-of-post.
notes.composite-key: Acceptable. (post_id, tag_id) PK prevents duplicate tagging.
notes.lookup-table: Acceptable. tags behaves as a reference table of allowed labels.
notes.unique-constraint: Acceptable. UNIQUE(name) on tags; or the composite PK on the junction.
notes.polymorphic-assoc: Poor. Tags apply to posts only here; a plain junction is right.

solution.notes: Classic many-to-many. Composite PK on post_tags stops a post being tagged with the same tag twice. UNIQUE(name) keeps the tag vocabulary clean.

title: Schema: Roles & permissions (RBAC)
prompt: Design role-based access control: users have roles, roles grant permissions. A user can have several roles and a role can include many permissions.

sources.label: Red-Gate â€” Top database schema design interview questions
InterviewQuery â€” Database design interview questions

keyIdea: Two many-to-many relationships â†’ two junction tables: user_roles and role_permissions. Roles and permissions are lookup tables; resolve a user's permissions by joining through roles.

notes.junction-table: Optimal. You need two: user_roles and role_permissions.
notes.composite-key: Optimal. (user_id, role_id) and (role_id, permission_id) as PKs prevent duplicates.
notes.index-fk: Optimal. Index the junction FKs for permission-resolution joins.
notes.lookup-table: Acceptable. roles and permissions are reference tables.
notes.unique-constraint: Acceptable. UNIQUE on role name / permission key.
notes.polymorphic-assoc: Poor. Standard junctions with real FKs are correct.
notes.denormalize: Trap. Don't precompute a user_permissions cache up front â€” derive via joins first.

solution.notes: A user's effective permissions = users â†’ user_roles â†’ role_permissions â†’ permissions. Two clean junctions; no permission is stored on the user directly.

title: Schema: Subscriptions & invoices
prompt: Design billing: customers subscribe to a plan; each subscription generates invoices over time; track subscription status changes and store money correctly.

sources.label: Hello Interview â€” Data modeling for system design interviews
Red-Gate â€” Top database schema design interview questions

keyIdea: customerâ†’subscriptionâ†’invoices are one-to-many; plans are a lookup; money is DECIMAL/cents; capture subscription state changes in a history table rather than only overwriting a status column.

notes.money-decimal: Optimal. Invoice amounts must be exact â€” DECIMAL or integer minor units.
notes.status-history: Optimal. Subscription lifecycle (trialingâ†’activeâ†’past_dueâ†’canceled) is worth keeping as history.
notes.lookup-table: Acceptable. plans is a reference table with price/interval.
notes.timestamptz: Acceptable. Period start/end and billing dates should be UTC timestamps.
notes.denormalize: Trap. A cached 'current_status' is fine alongside history, but don't denormalize totals prematurely.

solution.notes: subscription_events keeps the full state timeline; subscriptions.current_status is a cached convenience. Invoices are one-to-many under a subscription. Money is DECIMAL throughout.

title: Schema: Category hierarchy
prompt: Design product categories that nest arbitrarily deep (Electronics â†’ Phones â†’ Smartphones), where products belong to a category.

sources.label: GeeksforGeeks â€” Database design & modeling interview questions
InterviewQuery â€” Database design interview questions

keyIdea: Model the tree with a self-referencing parent_id on categories (adjacency list). Products reference a category via FK. Mention closure table / path enumeration if deep subtree queries are needed.

notes.self-referencing-fk: Optimal. categories.parent_id â†’ categories.id models arbitrary nesting.
notes.one-to-many-fk: Optimal. A category has many products and many child categories.
notes.check-constraint: Acceptable. Optionally guard against a category being its own parent.
notes.lookup-table: Acceptable framing â€” categories acts as a reference tree.
notes.junction-table: Poor here. A product in exactly one category is one-to-many, not many-to-many (only add a junction if products can be in multiple categories).

solution.notes: Adjacency list (parent_id) is the simplest tree. Root categories have parent_id NULL. For frequent deep-subtree reads, mention a closure table or materialized path as an upgrade â€” but don't build it prematurely.
